rulings.Case Title: NIKKI MARKMAN VS. YUN ZHEN WANG ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Defendant Ying Shens Responses To Plaintiffs Form Interrogatories, Set One, Numbers 2.5, 12.1 And 15.1; Request For Sanctions Against Defendant Ying Shen And Her Attorney Of Record, Stanley Shen, In The Amount Of $1,644.95
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, November 19, 2019, Line 1, PLAINTIFF NIKKI MARKMAN's Motion To Compel Defendant Ying Shens Responses To Plaintiffs Form Interrogatories, Set One, Numbers 2.5, 12.1 And 15.1; Request For Sanctions Against Defendant Ying Shen And Her Attorney Of Record, Stanley Shen, In The Amount Of $1,644.95 Pro Tem Judge Sanford Kingsley, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: This a wrongful eviction action based on an alleged owner move-in. This motion seeks further responses to Plaintiff's Form Interrogatories 2.5, 12.1 and 15.1 and sanctions of $1644.95 against Defendant and her counsel. The motion is unopposed. After reviewing the motion including the Separate Statement and the supporting Declaration, the motion is granted as follows: Defendant shall within 20 days serve a further response to: Interrogatory 2.5(c) specifying the specific dates she resided at each identified address); or a statement of her inability to do so in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure section 2030.220 (c). Interrogatory 12.1, withdrawing her objection to the term "incident" and providing the information requested in subparts (a)-(d) of that interrogatory. or a statement of her inability to do so in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure section 2030.220 (c). Interrogatory 15.1, providing the information requested in subparts (a)-(c) of that interrogatory. Monetary sanctions in the amount of $1644.95 are awarded against Defendant Ying Shan and her attorney of record, Stanley Shen. As the prevailing party, Plaintiff shall prepare a form of order recapitulating this tentative ruling and shall bring a copy of the proposed order to the hearing, if any. In the absence of a hearing, the prevailing party shall timely comply with Cal. R. Ct. 3.1312. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to skmediate@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ELIZABETH ANNE PLATT VS. ARCHIMEDES BANYA SF, LLC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Archimedes Banya Sf Llc Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiff Further Responses To Its First Set Of Form Interrogatories, Special Interrogatories And Requests For Production Of Documents; And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, November 19, 2019, Line 8, DEFENDANTS ARCHIMEDES BANYA SF, LLC. AND MIKHAIL BRODSKY'S Motion To Compel Plaintiff Further Responses To Its First Set Of Form Interrogatories, Special Interrogatories And Requests For Production Of Documents; And Request For Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Sanford Kingsley, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: This motion seeks further responses to Form Interrogatory 8.7, Special Interrogatories 2-4,15,23,26,27,31,33,34 and 40 as well as requiring the actual production of documents promised in the responses and verifications to the interrogatory responses and responses to the requests for production of documents. The motion also seeks $3825 in monetary sanctions against Plaintiff and Plaintiff's counsel. The motion is granted and Plaintiff shall within 20 days do the following: --produce all documents committed to be produced in the responses to the Request for Production of Documents; --provide verifications to Plaintiff's responses to the Form Interrogatories, to the Special Interrogatories, and to the Requests for Production of Documents; --provide full and complete responses to Special Interrogatories 2-4, 15,23,26-27,31,33,34& 40 and --provide a further response to Form Interrogatory 8.7. For reasons set forth in CCP section 2023.010 (d) and (h), the motion for an award of monetary sanctions is granted in the amount $3000 against Plaintiff and Plaintiff's Counsel, Rafael Crespo.

rulings.Case Title: JAMES WASEF VS. ORTEGA, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To File First Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, November 19, 2019, Line 3, PLAINTIFF JAMES WASEF's Motion For Leave To File First Amended Complaint. Plaintiff James Wasef's motion for leave to file first amended complaint is granted on the following conditions, to which plaintiff must agree to either orally at the hearing or in a writing sent to counsel for Defendant prior to the hearing: (1) Defendant may supplement its pending summary judgment motion to address the new allegations on 30 days' notice; and (2) Plaintiff shall stipulate to a continuance of the existing December 30, 2019 hearing on Defendant's motion for summary judgment to January 17, 2020, so that the motions may be heard concurrently. If Plaintiff does not agree to these conditions, the motion is denied. Granting the motion comports with California's liberal policy of allowing amendments to pleadings. (See, e.g., Howard v. County of San Diego (2010) 184 Cal.App.4th 1422, 1428 ["The policy favoring amendment is so strong that it is a rare case in which denial of leave to amend can be justified."].) The conditions on the granting of the motion eliminate any legal prejudice to Defendant by the granting of the motion. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: CRAIG K. MARTIN SR VS. MASOOD ORDIKHANI ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Plaintiff First Cause Of Action
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, November 19, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANT MASOOD ORDIKHANI, HARLAN KELLY, IVY FINE, BENJAMIN POOLE, MICHAL BROWN, SAN FRANCISCO PUBLIC UTILITIES COMMISSION, KATHY HOW's Motion To Strike Plaintiff First Cause Of Action. Defendants' motion to strike Plaintiff's first cause of action and claim for punitive damages is granted as unopposed. In his late-filed opposition, Plaintiff Craig K. Martin, Sr. concedes that "individuals cannot be held liable for discrimination," and does not contest Defendants' showing that the Government Claims Act forbids punitive damages as a remedy against public entities. Accordingly, the claims against all individual Defendants are stricken, as are the claims for punitive damages. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: CRAIG K. MARTIN SR VS. MASOOD ORDIKHANI ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, November 19, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANT MASOOD ORDIKHANI, HARLAN KELLY, IVY FINE, BENJAMIN POOLE, MICHAL BROWN, SAN FRANCISCO PUBLIC UTILITIES COMMISSION, KATHY HOW's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendants' demurrer to Plaintiff's Verified First Amended Complaint is sustained without leave to amend as to all causes of action other than the first and third causes of action against the City, as to which the demurrer is sustained with 20 days leave to amend. In his late-filed 4-page opposition, Plaintiff Craig K. Martin, Sr. does not respond meaningfully, or at all, to Defendants' demurrer to the second cause of action for negligent hiring, training, retention or supervision; the fourth cause of action for age discrimination; the fifth cause of action for libel per se; the sixth and seventh causes of action for intentional and negligent infliction of emotional distress; or the eighth and ninth causes of action for interference with prospective economic advantage. To the extent that the court can discern Plaintiff's position, he seeks to pursue only his claims for retaliation, harassment, and racial discrimination, which are included in the first cause of action, to which the City has not demurred, and the third cause of action. Those causes of action are defective because, among other things, Plaintiff does not explicitly allege that the allegedly harassing behavior by defendants Ordikhani or Poole was due to his race. Nor does he clearly allege what protected activity allegedly prompted the claimed retaliation. Plaintiff is granted 20 days leave to plead cognizable claims for relief, which shall be separately stated in accordance with Cal. Rules of Court, rule 2.112. If Plaintiff fails timely to file an amended complaint, the action will be dismissed. (Code Civ. Proc., § 581(f)(2).) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the City is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ANGES PAK VS. GITHUB, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, November 19, 2019, Line 10, DEFENDANT GITHUB, INC. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication. Defendant Github, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment is denied. Defendant Github, Inc.'s motion for summary adjudication is granted on the first cause of action for violation of the Equal Pay Act. To recover under Labor Code, § 1197.5(a) or (b), Plaintiff must demonstrate that she performed "substantially similar work, when viewed as a composite of skill, effort, and responsibility, and performed under similar working conditions." (Id; see also Hall v. County of Los Angeles (2007) 148 Cal.App.4th 318, 323 ["The Equal Pay Act exists to ensure that employees performing equal work are paid equal wages without regard to gender. To prove a violation of that basic principle, a plaintiff must establish that, based on gender, the employer pays different wages to employees doing substantially similar work under substantially similar conditions."].) To make out a prima facie case of discrimination under the Equal Pay Act, the plaintiff bears the burden of showing that the jobs being compared are "substantially equal." (Stanley v. University of Southern California (9th Cir. 1999) 178 F.3d 1069, 1074; see also Hall, 148 Cal.App.4th at 323 fn. 4 ["Because Labor Code section 1197.5 is substantively indistinguishable from its federal counterpart, California's courts rely on federal authorities construing the federal statute"].) The Court's full tentative ruling is sent by email to the parties. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which states that the motion was not opposed and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ALAN TORREZ VS. KWAN YIU DANIEL LEE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, November 19, 2019, Line 8, DEFENDANT KWAN YIU LEE's MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS. Off calendar. Defendant Kwan Yiu Daniel Lee did not serve the motion at least 16 court days before the hearing, plus five days for service by mail, as required. (Code Civ. Proc., § 1005(b).) In addition, the Court notes that Defendant's contention that to establish reasonable diligence, "service must be attempted at least three times, during three different times of day, at least three hours apart," is unsupported by any cited authority. "Two or three attempts to personally serve a defendant at a proper place ordinarily qualifies as 'reasonable diligence.'" (Rodriguez v. Cho (2015) 236 Cal.App.4th 742, 750.) =(302/EPS)
